perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties acknowledges that it is (i) aware, and has advised or will advise its Representatives, that the United States securities laws prohibit any person in possession of material non-public information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of such company, and from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person may purchase or sell such securities and (ii) familiar with the Securities and Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, and the rules and regulations thereunder, and each party agrees that it will neither use nor permit any of its Representatives to use any Confidential Information in violation of such Act or rules or regulations, including, without limitation, Rules 10b-5 and 14e-3.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties acknowledges that it is familiar with the general principles of fair market practices. Each party agrees that it will use its best efforts to ensure its Representatives act in accordance with these principles.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit acknowledgment of and agreement to abide by United States securities laws, including insider trading prohibitions. The new clause is vague and does not explicitly prohibit insider trading, which creates legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text replaces the specific acknowledgement of insider trading prohibitions under Rule 10b-5 with a general reference to 'fair market practices.' This weakens the commitment to complying with insider trading laws and could be interpreted as a failure to adequately address the legal obligations regarding material non-public information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly acknowledges and agrees to abide by United States securities laws, including insider trading prohibitions. The changed text replaces this specific acknowledgment with a general reference to 'fair market practices,' and removes the explicit prohibition of using confidential information in violation of securities laws, including Rules 10b-5 and 14e-3. This creates a legal loophole. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines Rule 10b-5 as prohibiting any act that operates as a fraud or deceit upon any person in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By removing the explicit reference to these laws, the changed text weakens the commitment to comply with insider trading laws. The omission of a legally mandated investor protection constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to waive, and to use its best efforts to cause its directors, officers, employees or agents to waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to consider waiving any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agrees to waive' to 'agrees to consider waiving', the contract no longer ensures a waiver of bond requirements for equitable relief. This could contradict state laws that prioritize preliminary injunctive relief where a bond waiver might be critical, especially in matters of trade secrets or confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 11 allows for injunctive relief in cases of unfair trade practices. Changing the language to 'agrees to consider' weakens the likelihood of obtaining injunctive relief without a bond, potentially undermining the effectiveness of Chapter 93A in protecting against misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates a waiver of bond requirements related to remedies, whereas the modified language only requires the parties to 'consider waiving' such requirements. This weakening directly undermines the potential for injunctive relief. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 11, allows for 'such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper' in cases of unfair trade practices. By merely agreeing to 'consider waiving' a bond, the modified contract introduces uncertainty, making it less likely that a party can obtain injunctive relief quickly and effectively, contradicting the intent of Chapter 93A.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and their respective directors, officers, employees, representatives and agents and their respective successors and assigns and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal substantive laws and not the choice of law rules of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction to be determined at the time of enforcement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to Massachusetts law and introducing ambiguity about the governing jurisdiction, the contract creates uncertainty about which state's laws will apply. This could violate consumer protection laws, as the original Massachusetts law provided a level of protection that might not exist in another, unspecified jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change removes the certainty of Massachusetts law applying, which includes the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (Chapter 93A). By leaving the governing law undetermined, the contract could be interpreted under a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protection laws, thereby contradicting the protections afforded by M.G.L. c. 93A.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit reference to Massachusetts law, including its consumer protection provisions (M.G.L. c. 93A), and replaces it with an ambiguous reference to a jurisdiction to be determined later. This creates a legal contradiction because it potentially subjects consumers to jurisdictions with weaker or non-existent consumer protection laws, directly undermining the protections offered by Massachusetts law as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Snippet 2, specifically concerning unfair business practices as stated in Chapter 93A Section 2. The omission of Massachusetts law and the uncertainty introduced contradict the consumer protection legally mandated by Massachusetts.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a Transaction is consummated, each party agrees that, for a period of eighteen (18) months from the date hereof, it will not hire or directly or indirectly solicit employment of (other than by means of a general advertisement) any of the (i) employees of the other party with whom it has had contact during the process contemplated by this Agreement or (ii) management level personnel or officers of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a Transaction is consummated, each party agrees that it will use commercially reasonable efforts not to hire employees of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 18-month non-solicitation clause and weakening the commitment to 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity and reduces protection against poaching employees. This could conflict with state laws that protect trade secrets and confidential information, as the non-solicitation clause served to prevent the potential misuse of such information by former employees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens protections against the misappropriation of trade secrets. Under the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42), preventing the solicitation of employees who possess trade secrets is a common protective measure. Removing the specific 18-month non-solicitation clause and weakening the language to 'commercially reasonable efforts' creates a potential conflict with the intent of MUTSA to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a specific 18-month non-solicitation clause, offering a defined period of protection against employee poaching. The changed text replaces this with a vague 'commercially reasonable efforts' standard. According to the Law Explanation, this weakens protections against the misappropriation of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA). The Perturbation Explanation highlights that removing the specific non-solicitation clause reduces protection against the misuse of confidential information by former employees. The scraped law snippets, specifically M.G.L. c. 93 § 42, define 'improper means' to include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information.' The original contract's 18-month non-solicitation clause directly addresses this, while the modified text provides substantially weaker protection and could be interpreted as insufficient to prevent such breaches, leading to a contradiction.
